summary: I was born in Israel in 1962, shortly after my parents immigrated here from Denmark. Growing up in Ra'anana, I experienced a blend of Israeli culture and the Danish traditions my parents kept alive at home. I served in the Israeli Army as an officer in a special unit, and my family's Zionist beliefs deeply influenced our life here. Despite spending some years back in Denmark during my childhood, Israel has always been my home and the center of my identity.

Denmark is where my parents were born and where they survived the Holocaust thanks to the Danish resistance movement. I spent part of my childhood there, attending a Jewish school and reconnecting with my Danish roots, including the language and culture. Although the move back to Denmark was difficult for me as a child, it was important for my parents to maintain our Danish heritage. We often visited family there, and my parents kept strong ties to the Danish Jewish community.

Copenhagen is the city where my parents were born and where they lived before and after the war. It was also where I attended a Jewish school at age eleven, which was my first real exposure to cooking classes. We had family there, including grandparents and cousins, and it was a place we visited frequently during my childhood. Although my time living there was challenging, Copenhagen remains a significant part of my family's history and my own upbringing.

My parents lived through the Holocaust in Denmark, where the Jewish community was saved by the Danish resistance movement and fishermen who helped them escape to neutral Sweden. This history deeply shaped my family's identity and their Zionist beliefs. Although my parents were very young during that time and did not know each other then, the Holocaust experience was a defining part of their lives and ours. It influenced their decision to immigrate to Israel and maintain Jewish traditions.

The Danish resistance movement played a crucial role in saving my parents and many other Danish Jews during the Holocaust by helping them escape to Sweden. This act of bravery and solidarity is well known and was a foundation of my family's survival story. My parents often spoke about how they were moved by fishermen to neutral Sweden, which was a lifeline during those dark times. Their gratitude and respect for the resistance shaped their Jewish identity and commitment to Zionism.

Sweden was the neutral country where my parents were taken by fishermen during the Holocaust to escape danger in Denmark. This safe haven was critical for their survival as young Jewish children. Later, our family took trips to Sweden during my childhood, along with visits to Norway, which helped me connect with the broader Scandinavian culture. Sweden represents a place of refuge and safety in my family's history.

My parents were committed Zionists who decided to immigrate to Israel in the early 1960s. They first came to Israel as young adults around eighteen to twenty years old, spending time on a kibbutz where they met and fell in love. Their Zionist beliefs motivated their move from Denmark to Israel, where they raised me with a strong Jewish identity. Zionism was a central part of our family's story and values.

My parents first came to Israel as young adults and spent time living on a kibbutz, which is where they met and fell in love. This communal agricultural lifestyle was part of their early Zionist experience in Israel before they returned to Denmark to finish their studies. The kibbutz was an important setting for their personal and ideological development, shaping their decision to settle in Israel permanently.

Although Danish was the primary language spoken at home during my childhood, I also learned Hebrew as part of growing up in Israel. My parents insisted on maintaining Danish at home, but Hebrew was naturally part of my education and daily life in Israel. This bilingual environment shaped my cultural identity, balancing my Danish heritage with my Israeli upbringing.

I grew up mostly in Ra'anana, Israel, where our family lived in a home filled with Danish culture and Jewish traditions. Ra'anana was a place of freedom and community, where I enjoyed playing outside and attending school. It was also where we observed Friday night rituals like Kiddush and Birkat Hamazon, surrounded by family and friends. Ra'anana represents my Israeli childhood and the blending of cultures in my life.

Kiddush was an important part of our Friday night family tradition growing up in Israel. My parents made sure we sat together to recite the prayer over the wine before our meal, creating a moment of connection and spirituality. Even though we were not strictly religious, Kiddush was a meaningful ritual that linked us to our Jewish heritage. It was one of the ways my family maintained Jewish customs in our home.

After our Friday night meals, we always said Birkat Hamazon, the blessing after eating, as part of our traditional Jewish practice. My father was strict about us sitting through the entire blessing, emphasizing respect for the ritual. This practice was a regular part of our family life in Ra'anana and reflected our commitment to Jewish customs, even in a liberal household. It helped instill a sense of spirituality and gratitude in me from a young age.

The Yom Kippur War in 1973 was a significant event during my childhood, coinciding with our time living in Denmark. When the war broke out, my father immediately flew back to Israel to teach, as many teachers were called to serve in the military. I remember feeling jealous that I wasn't in Israel with my friends during such a critical time. The war added to the challenges of living away from home and made us eager to return to Israel.

During my childhood, our family took trips to Norway along with visits to Sweden, which helped me connect with the broader Scandinavian region. These trips were part of our efforts to maintain ties with our Danish and Scandinavian roots while living in Israel. Norway was a place of family travel and exploration, contributing to my multicultural upbringing.

While Tel Aviv was not a central focus in my personal story, it is part of the broader Israeli context in which I grew up. My parents' immigration and life in Israel were connected to the greater Tel Aviv metropolitan area, including places like Ra'anana where we lived. Tel Aviv represents the vibrant urban environment of Israel that contrasts with my Danish heritage and the kibbutz experience.
